title: Hyponatremia

sections.title: Definition & Epidemiology
sections.content: Hyponatremia is serum sodium <135 mEq/L, the most common electrolyte disorder in hospitalized patients. It reflects water excess relative to sodium, not necessarily sodium deficiency.
keyPoints: Associated with increased mortality, longer LOS, falls, fractures
Symptoms correlate with rate of development more than absolute level
Acute (<48h) vs Chronic (>48h) - critical distinction for treatment

sections.title: Diagnostic Algorithm
sections.content: A systematic approach based on volume status and urine studies identifies the cause.

subsections.name: Step 1: Confirm True Hyponatremia (Check Serum Osmolality)
items: Low Osm (<280): TRUE hypotonic hyponatremia - proceed to step 2
Normal Osm (280-295): Pseudohyponatremia (lab artifact from high lipids/proteins)
High Osm (>295): Translocational - glucose pulls water into vascular space
Corrected Na = Measured Na + 1.6 × [(Glucose - 100)/100]

subsections.name: Step 2: Assess Volume Status
items: HYPOVOLEMIC: orthostatic hypotension, tachycardia, dry mucosa, poor skin turgor
EUVOLEMIC: no signs of volume excess or depletion
HYPERVOLEMIC: edema, ascites, JVD, pulmonary congestion

subsections.name: Step 3: Check Urine Studies
items: Urine Osmolality: >100 = impaired water excretion | <100 = appropriate dilution (psychogenic polydipsia, beer potomania)
Urine Sodium: >30 = renal salt wasting | <30 = extrarenal loss or edematous states

sections.title: Causes by Volume Status
sections.content: Once volume status is determined, specific causes can be identified.

subsections.name: Hypovolemic Hyponatremia (Na + water loss, water retained)
items: UNa >30: Renal losses - diuretics (especially thiazides), salt-wasting nephropathy, Addison's disease, cerebral salt wasting
UNa <30: Extrarenal losses - GI (vomiting, diarrhea), third-spacing, burns, sweating

subsections.name: Euvolemic Hyponatremia
items: SIADH (most common): UOsm >100, UNa >30, clinically euvolemic, uric acid low
Hypothyroidism: check TSH
Adrenal insufficiency: check cortisol (Addison's can mimic SIADH)
Psychogenic polydipsia: UOsm <100 (maximally dilute), history of excessive water intake
Beer potomania/tea-and-toast: low solute intake, UOsm <100

subsections.name: Hypervolemic Hyponatremia (Total body Na and water increased, but water > Na)
items: Heart failure: poor renal perfusion, ADH release
Cirrhosis: splanchnic vasodilation, effective arterial underfilling
Nephrotic syndrome: third-spacing, low oncotic pressure
Advanced kidney disease: inability to excrete free water
UNa typically <30 (except in kidney disease)

sections.title: SIADH: The Most Common Cause
sections.content: SIADH (Syndrome of Inappropriate ADH) is the most common cause of euvolemic hyponatremia. Diagnosis requires excluding hypothyroidism and adrenal insufficiency.
keyPoints: Diagnostic criteria: Serum Osm <275, UOsm >100, UNa >30, euvolemic, normal thyroid/adrenal
Causes (remember HEAD): Head trauma, Ectopic production (lung cancer), Adverse drug effects, Diseases (pulmonary, CNS)
Common drugs: SSRIs, carbamazepine, vincristine, cyclophosphamide, NSAIDs, opioids
Cancers: Small cell lung cancer most common, also pancreatic, lymphoma
Pulmonary: pneumonia, TB, COPD, positive pressure ventilation

sections.title: Treatment: Rate Matters!
sections.content: Treatment depends on symptom severity and chronicity. Overcorrection causes osmotic demyelination syndrome (ODS).

subsections.name: Acute Severe Symptomatic (<48h, seizures, coma)
items: Medical emergency - risk of brain herniation > risk of ODS
3% hypertonic saline 100-150mL IV bolus over 10-20 minutes
Goal: raise Na 4-6 mEq/L in first 1-2 hours to stop symptoms
Can repeat bolus 1-2 times if still symptomatic
Then slow correction: no more than 10 mEq/L in first 24h total

subsections.name: Chronic Asymptomatic/Mild Symptoms
items: Brain has adapted - SLOW correction mandatory
Goal: 4-6 mEq/L per 24 hours, max 8 mEq/L in any 24h period
High risk for ODS: Na <105, hypokalemia, alcoholism, malnutrition, liver disease
If high risk for ODS: limit to 4-6 mEq/L per 24h

subsections.name: Treatment by Volume Status
items: Hypovolemic: isotonic saline (0.9% NS) - volume repletes AND corrects Na
Euvolemic (SIADH): fluid restriction (800-1000mL/day), salt tabs, +/- tolvaptan (vaptans), demeclocycline, or loop diuretic + salt
Hypervolemic: fluid and sodium restriction, treat underlying disease, diuretics
AVOID hypotonic fluids in all cases

sections.title: Osmotic Demyelination Syndrome (ODS)
sections.content: ODS (formerly central pontine myelinolysis) occurs with overly rapid sodium correction. It causes devastating, often permanent neurologic injury.
keyPoints: Risk: chronic hyponatremia corrected too fast (>10-12 mEq/L in 24h)
Pathophysiology: brain adapts to low Na by losing organic osmolytes; rapid correction causes water shift OUT of brain cells
Symptoms: 2-6 days after correction - quadriplegia, pseudobulbar palsy, locked-in syndrome
Location: central pons (classic), but can be extrapontine
Treatment: if overcorrection detected, LOWER sodium back with D5W +/- desmopressin
Prevention: slow correction, frequent Na monitoring (q2-4h initially)

keyTakeaways: Hyponatremia is about WATER excess, not sodium deficiency
Step 1: Check serum Osm to confirm true hyponatremia
Step 3: Urine Osm (<100 = appropriate dilution) and UNa
SIADH = euvolemic + UOsm >100 + UNa >30 + normal thyroid/adrenal
Acute symptomatic: 3% saline bolus for emergency
Chronic: SLOW correction (4-8 mEq/L per 24h) to avoid ODS
Overcorrection is dangerous - can re-lower Na with D5W + desmopressin

reviewQuestions.question: A 70-year-old on HCTZ presents with Na 118, serum Osm 255, orthostatic hypotension, UNa 45, UOsm 500. What is the cause?
reviewQuestions.explanation: Hypovolemic (orthostatic) + high UNa (>30) = renal salt wasting. Thiazides are the classic cause. UNa is high because the kidney is inappropriately wasting sodium. Treat with NS to volume replete (and stop the thiazide!).

reviewQuestions.question: A patient with SIADH and Na 126 is treated aggressively. Na rises from 118 to 132 in 18 hours. You're concerned about ODS. What do you do?
options: Give D5W +/- desmopressin to re-lower sodium
reviewQuestions.explanation: This is overcorrection (14 mEq/L in 18h when goal is 8-10/24h). To prevent ODS, you must RE-LOWER sodium using D5W infusion +/- desmopressin (which causes water retention). The goal is to slow down the rate of correction.

reviewQuestions.question: Which finding distinguishes SIADH from hypovolemic hyponatremia?
reviewQuestions.explanation: Both SIADH and some hypovolemic causes (like diuretics) have high UNa and concentrated urine. The KEY distinction is clinical volume status: SIADH is euvolemic, while diuretics cause hypovolemia. Always assess volume status!

mcqs.stem: Which of the following is the MOST important initial step in evaluating a patient with suspected hyponatremia?

options.text: Consult a specialist before any workup
options.explanation: Initial evaluation should be performed by the treating physician; specialist consultation follows based on findings.

options.text: Obtain a thorough history and physical examination
options.explanation: A comprehensive history and physical examination is the foundation of clinical reasoning in hyponatremia. It guides all subsequent diagnostic and management decisions.
Treatment should generally follow an appropriate diagnostic evaluation unless the patient is critically unstable.
Advanced imaging may be indicated but should be guided by clinical findings, not ordered reflexively.

correct.reinforcement: Correct! Obtain a thorough history and physical examination.
correct.deeperWhy: Understanding this concept is foundational for clinical reasoning in this domain.
correct.clinicalPearl: Remember: Obtain a thorough history and physical examination

coachDx.misconception: Initial evaluation should be performed by the treating physician; specialist consultation follows based on findings.
coachDx.guidance: Review the key features that distinguish Obtain a thorough history and physical examination from Consult a specialist before any workup.
coachDx.bridgeTo: The key distinction involves Obtain a thorough history and physical examination.

mcqs.stem: A patient presents with classic features of hyponatremia. Which clinical reasoning principle is MOST important in avoiding diagnostic error?

options.explanation: Systematic consideration of differential diagnoses, including must-not-miss conditions, is essential for avoiding premature closure and anchoring bias.

options.text: Search satisficing - stop looking after the first diagnosis
options.explanation: Premature closure (stopping the diagnostic process too early) is a common cognitive error that leads to missed diagnoses.
While pattern recognition (System 1 thinking) is useful, it should be balanced with analytical reasoning (System 2) to avoid cognitive biases.

options.text: Order every available test to rule out all possibilities
options.explanation: Indiscriminate testing leads to false positives, unnecessary procedures, and increased cost without improving diagnostic accuracy.

correct.reinforcement: Correct! Consider the differential diagnosis systematically.
correct.deeperWhy: Understanding this concept is foundational for clinical reasoning in this domain.
correct.clinicalPearl: Remember: Consider the differential diagnosis systematically

coachDx.misconception: Indiscriminate testing leads to false positives, unnecessary procedures, and increased cost without improving diagnostic accuracy.
coachDx.guidance: Review the key features that distinguish Consider the differential diagnosis systematically from Order every available test to rule out all possibilities.
coachDx.bridgeTo: The key distinction involves Consider the differential diagnosis systematically.

mcqs.stem: When managing hyponatremia, which approach best supports evidence-based practice?

options.text: Base decisions solely on clinical experience
options.explanation: Clinical experience is valuable but can introduce bias; it should be combined with current evidence.

options.text: Follow institutional protocols regardless of patient factors
options.explanation: Evidence-based practice integrates best evidence with clinical expertise AND patient values/circumstances.

options.text: Always defer to the most recent published study
options.explanation: Individual studies should be appraised for quality and relevance; systematic reviews and guidelines generally provide stronger evidence.

options.text: Integrate current best evidence with clinical expertise and patient values
options.explanation: Evidence-based medicine is the conscientious integration of best research evidence, clinical expertise, and patient values in making clinical decisions.

correct.reinforcement: Correct! Integrate current best evidence with clinical expertise and patient values.
correct.deeperWhy: Understanding this concept is foundational for clinical reasoning in this domain.
correct.clinicalPearl: Remember: Integrate current best evidence with clinical expertise and patient values

coachDx.misconception: Individual studies should be appraised for quality and relevance; systematic reviews and guidelines generally provide stronger evidence.
coachDx.guidance: Review the key features that distinguish Integrate current best evidence with clinical expertise and patient values from Always defer to the most recent published study.
coachDx.bridgeTo: The key distinction involves Integrate current best evidence with clinical expertise and patient values.

mcqs.stem: A patient has the following ABG: pH 7.28, pCO2 24 mmHg, HCO3 11 mEq/L. Serum Na 140, Cl 100, K 4.0, albumin 4.0. What is the acid-base interpretation?

options.text: Anion gap metabolic acidosis with concurrent non-anion gap metabolic acidosis
options.explanation: Delta-delta ratio is ~1.3 (17/13), which falls in the 1-2 range indicating pure AGMA. A ratio <1 would suggest concurrent NAGMA.

options.text: Non-anion gap metabolic acidosis (NAGMA) with appropriate respiratory compensation
options.explanation: Anion gap = Na - (Cl + HCO3) = 140 - (100 + 11) = 29. This is elevated (normal 12 ± 4), indicating an anion gap metabolic acidosis, not NAGMA.

options.text: Mixed anion gap metabolic acidosis and respiratory alkalosis
options.explanation: The pCO2 of 24 falls within Winter's formula range (22.5-26.5), indicating appropriate compensation rather than a primary respiratory alkalosis.

options.text: Anion gap metabolic acidosis with appropriate respiratory compensation
options.explanation: AG = 140 - (100+11) = 29 (elevated). Expected pCO2 by Winter's formula = 1.5(11) + 8 ± 2 = 22.5-26.5. Actual pCO2 of 24 is within this range, confirming appropriate respiratory compensation. Calculate delta-delta: Δ AG = 29-12 = 17; Δ HCO3 = 24-11 = 13. Ratio ~1.3 suggests pure AGMA without concurrent non-AG acidosis or metabolic alkalosis.

correct.reinforcement: Correct! Anion gap metabolic acidosis with appropriate respiratory compensation.
correct.deeperWhy: Understanding this concept is foundational for clinical reasoning in this domain.
correct.clinicalPearl: Remember: Anion gap metabolic acidosis with appropriate respiratory compensation

coachDx.misconception: Delta-delta ratio is ~1.3 (17/13), which falls in the 1-2 range indicating pure AGMA. A ratio <1 would suggest concurrent NAGMA.
coachDx.guidance: Review the key features that distinguish Anion gap metabolic acidosis with appropriate respiratory compensation from Anion gap metabolic acidosis with concurrent non-anion gap metabolic acidosis.
coachDx.bridgeTo: The key distinction involves Anion gap metabolic acidosis with appropriate respiratory compensation.
